Press enter or click to view image in full size

Learn why PostgreSQL connections are expensive, why increasing max_connections isn’t the solution, and how PgBouncer helps production systems scale efficiently with connection pooling.

5 min read
Just now

--

Most developers don’t think about database connections until their application starts receiving real traffic.
During development, everything works perfectly with a handful of users. But as your application grows, the database often becomes the first bottleneck. Surprisingly, it’s not always because of slow queries. Sometimes, it’s simply because there are too many connections.
This is where understanding PostgreSQL’s connection architecture becomes important.
In this article, we’ll explore:

• How PostgreSQL handles connections

• Why database connections are expensive

• What happens when applications scale

• Why increasing max_connections is usually the wrong solution

• How PgBouncer solves the problem

• PgBouncer pooling modes

• Best practices for production

Understanding PostgreSQL’s Connection Model
Unlike many modern databases or web servers, PostgreSQL uses a process per connection architecture.
Every time a client connects to PostgreSQL, the operating system creates a dedicated backend process for that connection.
Client 1 ─────────► Backend Process #1Client 2 ─────────► Backend Process #2Client 3 ─────────► Backend Process #3Client 4 ─────────► Backend Process #4Notice that PostgreSQL creates one operating system process for every active connection.
This is very different from servers like Nginx or Node.js that can efficiently handle thousands of requests using threads or event loops.

Why Is Every Connection Expensive?
Every PostgreSQL connection consumes system resources.
Each backend process requires:

• Memory allocation

• File descriptors

• CPU scheduling

• Process management

• Context switching
Even if a client is idle, PostgreSQL still keeps that backend process alive.
Imagine 500 idle clients.
That’s still 500 backend processes.
Those processes continue consuming resources while doing absolutely nothing.

The Hidden Cost of Idle Connections
Many applications create connection pools.
For example:
Application Server
Pool Size = 100If you deploy four application servers:
Server 1 → 100 ConnectionsServer 2 → 100 ConnectionsServer 3 → 100 ConnectionsServer 4 → 100 ConnectionsTotal:
4 × 100 = 400 PostgreSQL ConnectionsHere’s the problem.
Most PostgreSQL installations are configured with:
max_connections = 100Your application now wants 400 connections.
PostgreSQL only allows 100.
The remaining 300 clients receive:
FATAL:
remaining connection slots are reservedor
too many clients already
The Architecture Without PgBouncer
Users
│
▼
Load Balancer
│
┌──────────────┼──────────────┐
▼ ▼ ▼
App Server 1 App Server 2 App Server 3
│ │ │
└──────────────┼──────────────┘
│
Hundreds of DB Connections
│
▼
PostgreSQLEvery application server maintains its own database connections.
As traffic grows, PostgreSQL receives more and more connections.
Eventually, it reaches its limit.

Why Not Just Increase
max_connections
?
This is one of the most common mistakes.
Developers often think:
“Let’s increase max_connections from 100 to 1000.”
Problem solved?
Not really.
Increasing max_connections means PostgreSQL now has to maintain 1000 backend processes.
That means:

• More RAM usage

• More CPU overhead

• More context switching

• Lower cache efficiency

• Worse performance
Simply increasing the limit usually delays the problem rather than solving it.

Enter PgBouncer
PgBouncer is a lightweight PostgreSQL connection pooler.
Instead of letting every application connection become a real PostgreSQL connection, PgBouncer sits between your application and the database.
Applications
│
1000 Client Connections
│
▼
PgBouncer
│
20 Active PostgreSQL Connections
│
▼
PostgreSQLThousands of application connections.
Only a small number of real PostgreSQL connections.
That’s the entire idea.

Production Architecture
Users
│
100,000 HTTP Requests/sec
│
▼
Load Balancer
│
┌───────────────┼───────────────┐
▼ ▼ ▼
App Server 1 App Server 2 App Server 3
│ │ │
└───────────────┼───────────────┘
│
1000 Database Client Connections
│
▼
PgBouncer
│
20 Active PostgreSQL Connections
│
▼
PostgreSQL DatabasePgBouncer accepts client connections.
When a query needs to execute, it borrows an available PostgreSQL connection from its pool.
After the query completes, that connection is returned to the pool and reused by another client.
This dramatically reduces the total number of backend processes PostgreSQL needs to maintain.

How PgBouncer Works
Imagine 1000 users.
Without PgBouncer:
1000 Users
│
▼
1000 PostgreSQL ConnectionsWith PgBouncer:
1000 Users
│
▼
PgBouncer
│
▼
20 PostgreSQL ConnectionsThose 20 connections are constantly reused.
Most web requests spend far more time waiting for network responses or application logic than actively executing SQL.
PgBouncer takes advantage of that idle time.

PgBouncer Pooling Modes
PgBouncer supports three pooling modes.
1. Session Pooling
Client connects↓Dedicated DB connection↓Connection remains reserved↓Client disconnectsSafest mode.
Works with everything.
Lowest pooling efficiency.

2. Transaction Pooling (Most Popular)
Client starts transaction↓Gets DB connection↓Transaction finishes↓Connection returned to poolHighest efficiency.
Used by most production applications.
This is generally the recommended mode for stateless web applications.

3. Statement Pooling
Every SQL statement↓Gets temporary connection↓Returns immediatelyHighest performance.
Least compatible.
Rarely used.

Things That Behave Differently
Transaction pooling has limitations.
Applications relying on session state need extra attention.
Examples include:

• Session variables (SET)

• Temporary tables

• Session level advisory locks

• Prepared statements (depending on client behavior)

• LISTEN / NOTIFY
Always verify your application’s compatibility before enabling transaction pooling.

Choosing the Right Pool Size
There isn’t a universal formula.
A common starting point is:
Initial Pool Size ≈ CPU Cores × 2This is only a heuristic.
The optimal pool size depends on:

• Query duration

• Request throughput

• PostgreSQL max_connections

• Available CPU

• Available memory

• Workload characteristics
The best approach is to monitor production metrics and tune accordingly.

Benefits of PgBouncer
Using PgBouncer provides several advantages:

• Lower memory usage

• Fewer PostgreSQL backend processes

• Faster connection handling

• Better scalability

• Reduced CPU overhead

• Improved throughput

• Protection against connection exhaustion

• More stable production systems

When Should You Use PgBouncer?
PgBouncer is a great fit when:

• You have multiple application servers.

• Your application receives high traffic.

• You run PostgreSQL in production.

• You experience “too many connections” errors.

• You use microservices.

• You deploy on Kubernetes.

• You use serverless functions with PostgreSQL.

• You want better database resource utilization.

When Might You Not Need It?
For a small application with:

• One application server

• Few concurrent users

• Low traffic

• Minimal database activity
PgBouncer may not provide noticeable benefits.
As your application scales, however, it often becomes a valuable part of your architecture.

Common Misconceptions
“More connections mean better performance.”
Not necessarily.
Beyond a certain point, additional connections can reduce performance due to increased process management and context switching.
“Increasing
max_connections
solves scaling.”
It often postpones the issue rather than addressing it.
“PgBouncer makes queries faster.”
PgBouncer doesn’t optimize SQL queries. It reduces the overhead of establishing and managing database connections, allowing PostgreSQL to spend more time executing queries.

Final Thoughts
Database performance isn’t only about writing efficient SQL queries. It’s also about managing database connections effectively.
PostgreSQL’s process per connection architecture is robust and reliable, but each connection comes with a cost. As applications scale, unmanaged connection growth can quickly become a bottleneck.
PgBouncer addresses this by allowing thousands of client connections to share a much smaller pool of real PostgreSQL connections. The result is lower memory usage, reduced overhead, and a database that scales more efficiently under load.
If your first instinct is to increase max_connections, consider asking a different question:
Do you actually need more PostgreSQL connections, or do you need better connection management?
For many production systems, the answer is PgBouncer.
